status_note: Focused on the rpc.go two-writer path, HandlerAdapter, and the B1 lost-update lock. Read rpc.go in full, append.go/state.go in full, and the Persist/Load atomic-write helpers in persistence.go.

findings.title: queue-submit read-modify-write bypasses the B1 mutation lock (TOCTOU single-active + lost update)
findings.detail: HandleQueueSubmit performs Load(existing) -> Validate (QM-027 single-active-per-name guard) -> Persist entirely UNLOCKED. The B1 fix (appendUnderLock, LockForMutationView) serialised only the append path against the workloop's LockForMutation status mutations; submit was never brought under the same lock. Two concrete failures: (1) TOCTOU on QM-027 — two concurrent queue-submit calls for the same new queue name both Load (see no active queue), both pass the single-active check, both mint distinct queue_ids, and both Persist to the same <name>.json path; last-writer-wins silently drops one queue's items and queue_id, defeating the single-active-per-name invariant. (2) A submit's Persist can race the workloop's locked status Persist for a name that is transitioning, so the workloop's in-flight status write and the submit can clobber each other's file. The adapter then calls a.qs.SetQueue (rpc.go:1004) also outside the lock.
findings.suggested_fix: Route HandleQueueSubmit's load->validate->persist->SetQueue through the same MutationLocker.LockForMutationView path used by appendUnderLock, so the single-active check and the write are atomic w.r.t. the workloop and concurrent submits.

findings.title: Persist temp-file name keyed only on PID — concurrent in-process writes to the same queue collide
findings.detail: tmpPath := fmt.Sprintf("%s.tmp-%d", target, os.Getpid()) uses only the PID as the uniquifier. Two concurrent Persist calls in the SAME daemon process targeting the SAME queue name produce the identical tmpPath. Because the file is opened O_CREATE|O_EXCL, the second call fails with ErrPersistFailed even though nothing is actually wrong — a spurious internal-error (-32099) returned to the caller. This is reachable precisely because the submit path is unlocked (finding above): an unlocked submit Persist and a locked append/workloop Persist for the same name can overlap. Even absent that, any future concurrent writer to one queue name hits it.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a per-call uniquifier to tmpPath (e.g. a random token / atomic counter / UUID) in addition to the PID so concurrent Persist attempts never collide on the temp filename.

findings.title: Unbounded group-index access in the append path can panic instead of returning append_target_invalid
findings.detail: HandleQueueAppendOnQueue does targetGroup := mutated.Groups[req.GroupIndex] with no bounds check, relying entirely on AppendItems/Validate having rejected an out-of-range GroupIndex first. AppendItems itself dereferences q.Groups[groupIndex] at append.go:124 (existingGroup) and append.go:166 (g := &q.Groups[groupIndex]) — also unchecked. req.GroupIndex arrives directly from decoded JSON. If the Validate pipeline ever fails to reject an out-of-range or negative index (regression, or a code path where GroupIndex validation is skipped), the daemon panics on an index-out-of-range instead of returning the typed append_target_invalid error, taking down the socket handler goroutine.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an explicit `if req.GroupIndex < 0 || req.GroupIndex >= len(q.Groups) { return append_target_invalid }` guard before indexing, in both HandleQueueAppendOnQueue and AppendItems, as defense-in-depth independent of the validation pipeline.

findings.title: HandleQueueSetConcurrency doc claims 'validates N >= 1' but no such validation exists
findings.detail: The doc comment states the handler 'validates N >= 1'. The body never checks req.N; a zero or negative N flows straight into the spawn-cap arithmetic req.N*2 > spawnCap (rpc.go:1337) and only later is rejected by a.concurrencySet(req.N) — if wired. For a negative N, req.N*2 stays <= spawnCap so the spawn-cap guard is silently skipped, and correctness depends entirely on the concurrencySet setter validating. A very large N makes req.N*2 an integer-overflow risk in the cap comparison (theoretical on 64-bit). Doc/impl drift plus reliance on an optional collaborator for input validation.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an explicit `if req.N < 1 { return invalid_concurrency }` at the top of the handler, matching the documented contract, and validate before the spawn-cap multiply.

findings.title: appendUnderLock disk-fallback safety comment relies on an invariant that submit violates
findings.detail: The disk-fallback branch (q == nil -> resolveAppendTargetFromDisk) is annotated 'still safe because every writer persists under this same lock.' That invariant is false: HandleQueueSubmit persists WITHOUT taking LockForMutationView (see the high finding). So a locked append that fell back to a disk Load can read a queue that an unlocked concurrent submit is simultaneously rewriting, then persist a stale-derived mutation. The comment gives false assurance to future maintainers.
findings.suggested_fix: Once submit is brought under the mutation lock, the comment becomes true; until then, correct the comment to note submit is an unlocked writer, or eliminate the disk fallback for in-daemon callers.

findings.title: Event payload marshal errors silently drop lifecycle events
findings.detail: In HandleQueueSubmit adapter, `if raw, err := json.Marshal(payload); err == nil { _ = a.bus.Emit(...) }` — a marshal failure silently skips emitting queue_submitted (and the same pattern at rpc.go:1037 for queue_item_deferred_for_ledger_dep). A consumer waiting on queue_submitted (e.g. the workloop / a subscriber) would never see it, with no log line explaining why. Marshal of these fixed-shape payloads rarely fails, but the swallow is invisible when it does.
findings.suggested_fix: Log to stderr (the daemon diagnostic channel) on marshal failure rather than silently discarding the event.

findings.title: Dead computed-and-discarded expression in AppendItems deferred-event loop
findings.detail: `_ = tailStart + i // index of the newly appended item; informational` computes a value and immediately discards it with a blank assignment. It has no effect and no downstream use; tailStart exists only to feed this dead line.
findings.suggested_fix: Delete the line and the now-unused tailStart local (or actually use the index if it was meant to populate the event).
